title: Why Central Asia Is Turning to Putin for Nuclear Power
gist: The Diplomat frames Central Asia's push into AI and critical-minerals mining as colliding with an aging, under-invested Soviet-era power grid, pushing the region's largest economies toward nuclear power built in partnership with Russia — raising the question of whether this trades one crisis for a new geopolitical dependency.

insights.claim: Central Asia's push into artificial intelligence and critical-minerals mining is colliding with an energy shortfall: decades of under-investment plus a booming population have left the region's aging Soviet-era power infrastructure unable to meet surging demand, producing blackouts, rationing, and temporary restrictions as a seasonal reality.
insights.speaker: The Diplomat
insights.so_what: Signals a hard infrastructure constraint on Central Asia's AI and mining investment thesis: power availability, not capital, is the binding constraint on growth.
insights.locator: Central Asia is trapped in an energy paradox

insights.claim: To close the energy gap, Central Asia's largest economies are turning to nuclear power as their plan to keep the lights on, which in practice means partnering with Russia.
insights.speaker: The Diplomat
insights.so_what: Identifies Russia re-entering as the region's energy-infrastructure supplier of record, a geopolitical dependency to monitor alongside China's existing regional investment footprint.
insights.locator: the region's largest economies have a new plan

insights.claim: The piece frames Central Asia's nuclear turn to Moscow as an open question — whether partnering with Russia is a workable solution or a geopolitical trap that could replace the current energy crisis with a new, deeper dependency.
insights.speaker: The Diplomat
insights.so_what: Flags counterparty and geopolitical risk for any Central Asia nuclear-buildout thesis: Russian-built nuclear plants create decades-long technical and political dependency beyond the initial construction deal.
insights.quote: is partnering with Moscow a brilliant solution, or a geopolitical trap?
insights.locator: And that raises a massive question
